paper_metadata.authors: Abdulkadiroglu, Atila; Angrist, Joshua D.; Narita, Yusuke; Pathak, Parag A.
paper_metadata.summary: This paper develops methods to identify causal effects of school assignments in centralized matching schemes that use a mix of random lottery and non-lottery tie-breaking. The authors focus on the New York City public school district, which employs criteria like test scores and interviews for applicant rankings at some schools, combined with lottery tie-breaking at others. The paper generalizes the standard regression discontinuity design to accommodate multiple running variables and treatments, some randomly assigned. The study finds that while Grade A schools in NYC boost achievement, these gains are evident only for students attending lottery schools. Attendance at Grade A screened schools, some of the highest performing, shows no measurable effects. The paper highlights the importance of leveraging both lottery and non-lottery variations to accurately evaluate school impacts.

ccas_systems.notes: New York City uses a mix of random lottery and non-lottery criteria such as test scores and interviews for school assignments. The system is coordinated, with a centralized algorithm generating single offers based on applicant preferences and school priorities.

paper_metadata.title: Research Design Meets Market Design: Using Centralized Assignment for Impact Evaluation
paper_metadata.authors: Abdulkadiroğlu, Atila; Angrist, Joshua D.; Narita, Yusuke; Pathak, Parag A.
paper_metadata.summary: This paper explores the use of centralized assignment mechanisms in school districts to allocate school seats, reflecting student preferences and school priorities. The authors develop empirical strategies to exploit the random assignment embedded in these mechanisms, particularly in oversubscribed schools where lotteries are used. The study focuses on Denver, where charter and traditional public schools are combined in a unified assignment system, revealing significant achievement gains from charter school attendance. The paper also discusses the benefits of centralized matching schemes like Deferred Acceptance (DA), which improve welfare and transparency while reducing strategic manipulation. The authors highlight the potential of these systems for quasi-experimental research designs to evaluate school effectiveness and reform models.

ccas_systems.notes: Denver uses a unified assignment system combining charter and traditional public schools, employing Deferred Acceptance to allocate seats. The system allows for quasi-experimental evaluation of school effectiveness, particularly in oversubscribed schools where lotteries are used.

paper_metadata.title: Efficiency, Justified Envy, and Incentives in Priority-Based Matching
paper_metadata.authors: Abdulkadiroğlu, Atila, Che, Yeon-Koo, Pathak, Parag A, Roth, Alvin E, Tercieux, Olivier
paper_metadata.summary: This paper examines the efficiency and fairness of the Top Trading Cycles (TTC) mechanism in priority-based matching systems, comparing it to other mechanisms such as Deferred Acceptance (DA) and Serial Dictatorship (SD). The authors demonstrate that TTC minimizes justified envy among all Pareto-efficient and strategy-proof mechanisms in one-to-one matching scenarios. In many-to-one matching, TTC is shown to have less justified envy than SD on average. The study uses empirical data from New Orleans OneApp and Boston Public Schools to illustrate that TTC results in significantly fewer instances of justified envy compared to SD. The paper also discusses the trade-offs between efficiency and fairness in these mechanisms and highlights the limited practical use of TTC due to the complexity of its priority system.

ccas_systems.notes: New Orleans uses the OneApp system, which employs the Top Trading Cycles mechanism to minimize justified envy in school assignments.
Boston Public Schools considered using the Top Trading Cycles mechanism for its school assignment system to balance priorities and choice.

paper_metadata.title: School Assignment by Match Quality
paper_metadata.authors: Abdulkadiroglu, Atila; Dur, Umut M.; Grigoryan, Aram
paper_metadata.summary: This paper challenges the effectiveness of school choice systems by highlighting that parents may not prioritize school effectiveness or match quality when selecting schools for their children. The authors propose novel algorithms to enhance match quality while maintaining stability constraints in school assignments. These algorithms are applicable to both small and large school districts and demonstrate significant improvements in match quality over the Deferred Acceptance mechanism with a random tie-breaker. The methodology can also be adapted to optimize other policy objectives in school choice and priority-based matching problems.

paper_metadata.title: Breaking Ties: Regression Discontinuity Design Meets Market Design
paper_metadata.authors: Abdulkadiroğlu, Atila, Angrist, Joshua D., Narita, Yusuke, Pathak, Parag
paper_metadata.summary: This paper explores the use of centralized school assignment algorithms in large urban districts, focusing on the New York City public high school match. The study generalizes regression discontinuity methods to accommodate multiple treatments and running variables, some randomly assigned, through a local propensity score that quantifies school assignment probabilities. The paper demonstrates the utility of this approach by assessing the predictive value of New York City's school report cards, finding that schools with higher grades improve SAT math scores and graduation rates, though less than OLS estimates suggest. The study highlights the role of deferred acceptance algorithms and lottery tie-breaking in creating quasi-experimental variation in school assignments.

ccas_systems.notes: New York City uses a centralized school assignment system for public high schools, employing a deferred acceptance algorithm. The system uses both test scores and lottery numbers as tie-breakers to rank applicants, creating a quasi-experimental setting for research on school value-added.

paper_metadata.title: Regression Discontinuity in Serial Dictatorship: Achievement Effects at Chicago’s Exam Schools
paper_metadata.authors: Abdulkadiroğlu, Atila, Angrist, Joshua D., Narita, Yusuke, Pathak, Parag A., Zarate, Roman A.
paper_metadata.summary: This paper introduces a hybrid regression discontinuity (RD) and propensity score empirical strategy to evaluate the effects of Chicago's exam schools on student achievement. It focuses on the serial dictatorship (SD) mechanism, which is commonly used for college and selective K–12 admissions, where applicants are assigned to their most preferred schools based on exam scores. The study highlights the challenges of using non-lottery tiebreaking criteria, such as test scores, which correlate with applicants' potential outcomes. The authors propose an RD-SD propensity score to control for the local probability of school assignment, aiming to eliminate omitted variables bias (OVB) in comparisons of school effects. The paper provides a detailed characterization of the SD mechanism and derives a large market propensity score for SD mechanisms with nonrandom tiebreakers.

ccas_systems.notes: Chicago's exam schools use a serial dictatorship mechanism with exam-score tie-breaking. Applicants are assigned to schools based on their exam scores and preferences. The system is characterized by admissions cutoffs and a propensity score that controls for assignment risk.

paper_metadata.title: Loss aversion in strategy-proof school-choice mechanisms
paper_metadata.authors: Meisner, Vincent; von Wangenheim, Jonas
paper_metadata.summary: The paper explores the impact of expectation-based loss aversion on strategy-proof school-choice mechanisms, such as deferred acceptance (DA) and top-trading cycles (TTC). It introduces the concept of choice-acclimating personal equilibria (CPE) to explain why participants might deviate from truthful preference submissions, which are theoretically optimal. The authors find that non-truthful preference submissions can be optimal if they satisfy a property called top-rank monotonicity. The study reveals that loss aversion can lead to inefficiencies and justified envy in equilibrium, as students with higher loss aversion may avoid ranking popular schools to mitigate disappointment, resulting in suboptimal allocations. The paper challenges the assumption that strategy-proof mechanisms level the playing field, highlighting that students' private information about their match values and loss aversion levels can influence their preference reports. This has implications for affirmative action policies and the empirical identification of true preferences in strategy-proof mechanisms.

paper_metadata.title: Closing the gap: Effect of a gender quota on women’s access to education in Afghanistan
paper_metadata.authors: Najam, Rafiuddin
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates the impact of a gender quota on women's access to higher education in Afghanistan, using data from public universities between 2013 and 2018. The study employs difference-in-differences estimators to assess the causal effects of the quota, which increased the proportion of women in treated groups by nine percentage points and the share of women from low socio-economic backgrounds by three percentage points. The quota led to a reduction in the score threshold for women's admission, particularly in competitive fields, where the share of women increased by 17 percentage points. The findings suggest that affirmative action can effectively address gender disparities in fragile settings.

ccas_systems.notes: Afghanistan's public university admissions are determined by the Kankor examination scores. In 2016, a gender quota was introduced to allocate seats by gender, creating gender-specific competition within certain concentrations.

paper_metadata.title: A Dynamic Matching Mechanism for College Admissions: Theory and Experiment
paper_metadata.authors: Gong, Binglin; Liang, Yingzhi
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates a dynamic matching mechanism used in college admissions, focusing on its application in Inner Mongolia, China. The authors theoretically and experimentally analyze the mechanism's efficiency and stability compared to the Deferred Acceptance (DA) and Boston mechanisms. They find that while the dynamic mechanism is less stable than DA and less efficient than the Boston mechanism in low-complexity settings, it matches their performance in high-complexity settings. This suggests the dynamic mechanism's resilience to complexity due to its belief-independent strategies. The study highlights potential applications beyond college admissions, such as housing assignments and job allocations.

ccas_systems.notes: The IM Dynamic mechanism allows students to submit one college choice at a time and revise it before a preannounced end time. The system displays real-time data on quotas, applicants, and scores. At the end, colleges accept students up to their quotas based on standardized test scores.

paper_metadata.title: From Immediate Acceptance to Deferred Acceptance: Effects on School Admissions and Achievement in England
paper_metadata.authors: Terrier, Camille; Pathak, Parag A.; Ren, Kevin
paper_metadata.summary: This paper examines the impact of England's 2008 national ban on the Immediate Acceptance (IA) school assignment mechanism, which was replaced by the Deferred Acceptance (DA) mechanism. The study uses a difference-in-differences research design to analyze the effects on school admissions and achievement, particularly focusing on socioeconomic status (SES) disparities. The findings indicate that the transition from IA to DA reduced school quality for low-SES students more than for high-SES students, as low-SES students ended up attending schools with lower value-added and more disadvantaged peers. This was primarily due to a decrease in low-SES admissions at selective schools. The paper suggests that DA, by encouraging high-SES parents to report preferences truthfully, increased competition for top schools, thereby crowding out low-SES students.

ccas_systems.notes: The paper discusses the national transition from Immediate Acceptance to Deferred Acceptance in 2008, affecting 16 local authorities that previously used IA. The reform aimed to reduce the disadvantage faced by families less familiar with strategic admissions processes.

paper_metadata.title: An Iterative Deferred Acceptance Mechanism for Decentralized, Fast and Fair Childcare Assignment
paper_metadata.authors: Reischmann, Tobias, Klein, Thilo, Giegerich, Sven
paper_metadata.summary: The paper introduces an iterative deferred acceptance mechanism with ties (IDAT) for childcare assignment in two German cities. The mechanism is designed to accommodate complementarities in providers' preferences, ensuring fast and stable allocations. It allows for a mix of centralized and decentralized participation, with some facilities submitting rank-order lists while others make independent offers. The IDAT mechanism aims to address inefficiencies in current childcare allocation practices, which are often uncoordinated and lead to unfair admissions. The proposed system automates acceptance decisions and public facilities' offer decisions, potentially speeding up the process and reducing the need for early, unattractive offers. The paper suggests that this model could serve as an intermediate step towards fully centralized childcare assignment systems.

ccas_systems.notes: The IDAT mechanism is applied to childcare assignment, allowing for a mix of centralized and decentralized participation. It automates acceptance decisions and public facilities' offer decisions, aiming to speed up the process and reduce unfair admissions.

paper_metadata.title: Efficient Matching in the School Choice Problem
paper_metadata.authors: Reny, Philip J.
paper_metadata.summary: The paper discusses the inefficiencies in stable matchings within school choice systems, highlighting that such matchings are not necessarily Pareto efficient. It introduces the concept of priority-neutral and priority-efficient matchings, showing that a unique priority-efficient matching exists that dominates all priority-neutral and stable matchings. The paper argues that truth-telling is a maxmin optimal strategy for students in the mechanism selecting the priority-efficient matching. The study examines the practical implications of these theoretical findings, citing examples from U.S. cities where significant inefficiencies occur in practice. It critiques existing mechanisms, such as the student-optimal stable matching, for their potential inefficiencies and explores alternative approaches, including modifications to the deferred acceptance algorithm and the use of legal sets of matchings.

ccas_systems.notes: The paper discusses inefficiencies in the student-optimal stable matching in New York City, where over 4,000 grade 8 students could have been better off with a different assignment. The system uses priority rules and lotteries to resolve conflicts.
Boston is mentioned as having a school choice system with priority rules and lotteries, similar to other U.S. cities. The paper critiques the inefficiencies in stable matchings.

paper_metadata.title: Minimally unstable Pareto improvements over deferred acceptance
paper_metadata.authors: Doğan, Battal; Ehlers, Lars
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates efficient and minimally unstable Pareto improvements over the deferred acceptance (DA) mechanism, a popular school choice mechanism known for its stability but not efficiency. The authors explore the possibility of achieving Pareto improvements over DA that are minimally unstable among efficient assignments. They find that no such Pareto improvement exists when stability is measured by counting blocking pairs. The paper characterizes priority profiles where a Pareto improvement over DA is minimally unstable among efficient assignments. The authors also consider an alternative stability comparison based on blocking students and find similar impossibilities. The main result highlights the conditions under which a Pareto improvement over DA can be cardinally minimally unstable among efficient assignments, providing insights into the restricted nature of such priority profiles and the reasons for potential failures in achieving cardinal minimal instability.

paper_metadata.title: Weak stability and Pareto efficiency in school choice
paper_metadata.authors: Tang, Qianfeng; Zhang, Yongchao
paper_metadata.summary: This paper explores the trade-off between stability and student welfare in school choice problems. It introduces the concept of weak stability, where a matching is weakly stable if none of its blocking pairs can be matched in a more stable matching. The paper also defines self-constrained efficiency and self-constrained optimality, which capture the balance between stability and efficiency. The efficiency-adjusted deferred acceptance mechanism (EADAM) is central to the discussion, as it Pareto improves on the deferred acceptance (DA) matching for students. The main theorem shows that a matching is weakly stable and self-constrained efficient if and only if it is self-constrained optimal and an EADAM outcome under some consenting constraints. The paper provides theoretical justifications for using EADAM in practice, highlighting its compatibility with Pareto efficiency.

ccas_systems.notes: The paper discusses the use of the deferred acceptance algorithm and its Pareto efficiency in the context of New York City's high school admissions.

paper_metadata.title: Costly information acquisition in centralized matching markets
paper_metadata.authors: Hakimov, Rustamdjan; Kübler, Dorothea; Pan, Siqi
paper_metadata.summary: The paper examines how costly information acquisition affects student welfare in centralized matching markets, particularly in university admissions. It compares sequential and direct serial dictatorship mechanisms, finding that the sequential mechanism improves student welfare by informing students about which universities are likely to admit them, thus guiding their search more effectively. Theoretical and experimental analyses show that the sequential mechanism reduces deviations from optimal search strategies. Additionally, providing historical cutoff scores under the direct mechanism can enhance welfare, especially when information costs are high, though this effect is weaker than that of the sequential mechanism. The study highlights the importance of designing admission procedures that minimize unnecessary information acquisition.

ccas_systems.notes: University admissions are based on a single exam where universities rank students similarly, aligning priorities across institutions.
Similar to China, university admissions are based on a single exam with aligned priorities.
University admissions use a single exam or GPA-based ranking system.
Admissions are based on a single exam or GPA, with aligned university priorities.
University admissions are based on a single exam with aligned priorities.
Admissions are based on a single exam or GPA, with aligned priorities across universities.

paper_metadata.title: School Choice with Consent: An Experiment
paper_metadata.authors: Cerrone, Claudia; Hermstrüwer, Yoan; Kesten, Onur
paper_metadata.summary: This paper explores the efficiency-adjusted deferred acceptance mechanism (EADAM) in school choice, which allows students to waive priorities that do not affect their assignments. The study provides experimental evidence comparing EADAM with the traditional deferred acceptance (DA) mechanism. Findings indicate that EADAM improves both efficiency and truth-telling rates compared to DA, despite not being strategy-proof. When priority waivers are enforced, efficiency increases further, though truth-telling rates decrease. The study challenges the necessity of strategy-proofness for truth-telling and highlights a new trade-off between efficiency and vulnerability to preference manipulation. The Flemish Ministry of Education in Belgium has begun implementing EADAM, motivated by its potential to balance efficiency and stability.

ccas_systems.notes: Flanders is implementing the efficiency-adjusted deferred acceptance mechanism (EADAM) to improve efficiency and stability in school assignments. EADAM allows students to waive certain priorities, potentially increasing overall assignment efficiency without harming individual welfare.

paper_metadata.title: Can Introducing Single-Sex Education into Low-Performing Schools Improve Academics, Arrests, and Teen Motherhood?
paper_metadata.authors: Jackson, C. Kirabo
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates the impact of converting coeducational schools to single-sex schools in Trinidad and Tobago. In 2010, the Ministry of Education converted 20 low-performing secondary schools to single-sex institutions. The study uses this policy change to assess the causal effects of single-sex education on various outcomes, including academic performance, arrests, and teen motherhood. The findings indicate that boys in single-sex cohorts scored higher on national exams, both boys and girls took more advanced coursework, and girls performed better on secondary-school completion exams. Additionally, all-boys cohorts had fewer arrests, and all-girls cohorts had lower teen pregnancy rates. The study suggests that these outcomes are due to both direct gender peer effects and changes in teacher behavior. This research contributes to the literature by providing evidence from a large-scale policy experiment, contrasting with previous studies that often relied on cross-school comparisons or smaller samples.

paper_metadata.title: Single-Sex Schools, Student Achievement, and Course Selection: Evidence from Rule-Based Student Assignments in Trinidad and Tobago
paper_metadata.authors: Jackson, C. Kirabo
paper_metadata.summary: This paper examines the impact of single-sex schooling on student achievement and course selection in Trinidad and Tobago, where students are assigned to secondary schools based on an algorithm. This setting allows for the estimation of the causal effects of attending single-sex schools by addressing self-selection bias. The study finds that while students, particularly females, with strong preferences for single-sex schools benefit, most students do not perform better at single-sex schools. Additionally, girls in single-sex schools tend to take fewer science courses and more traditionally female subjects. The paper contributes to the debate on the effectiveness of single-sex education by providing empirical evidence from a context where about a quarter of public secondary schools are single-sex, allowing for a comparison with coeducational schools that are similar in key aspects.

ccas_systems.notes: In Trinidad and Tobago, students are assigned to secondary schools using an algorithm. This system allows for the estimation of causal effects of single-sex schooling by addressing self-selection bias. The study focuses on the impact of single-sex schooling on student achievement and course selection.

paper_metadata.title: The Short and Long-Run Effects of Attending The Schools that Parents Prefer
paper_metadata.authors: Beuermann, Diether W.; Jackson, C. Kirabo
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates the effects of attending preferred public secondary schools on both short-term and long-term outcomes. Using a regression discontinuity design and data from Barbados, the study finds that while attending these schools does not significantly improve short-term test scores, it does lead to better long-term outcomes such as higher post-secondary completion rates and improved adult well-being. These benefits are particularly pronounced for females, who also experience reduced rates of teen motherhood. The study suggests that parents may prefer these schools not for immediate academic gains but for their potential to improve broader life outcomes. The paper also conducts a meta-analysis across various countries, finding that the lack of short-term test score improvements is a common phenomenon, which may be due to parents valuing non-academic benefits.

ccas_systems.notes: In Barbados, students take the BSSEE at the end of primary school and submit a ranked list of preferred secondary schools. The Ministry of Education uses a serial dictatorship algorithm to assign students based on their preferences and test scores.

paper_metadata.title: What is a Good School, and Can Parents Tell? Evidence on the Multidimensionality of School Output
paper_metadata.authors: Beuermann, Diether W.; Jackson, C. Kirabo; Navarro-Sola, Laia; Pardo, Francisco
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates whether schools' impacts on test scores reflect their overall impact on students by examining data from Trinidad and Tobago. The study estimates the causal impacts of individual schools on various outcomes, including test scores, arrests, dropout rates, teen motherhood, and labor market participation. It finds that schools' impacts on high-stakes tests are weakly related to other important outcomes. The paper also explores parental preferences for schools, linking them to parents' ranked lists of schools to infer preferences using discrete-choice models. It reveals that parents choose schools that improve high-stakes tests and reduce criminality and teen motherhood, with preferences varying between parents of low-achieving and high-achieving students. The findings suggest that evaluations based solely on test scores may mislead the benefits of school choice, especially for low-achieving students.

ccas_systems.notes: The study uses data from Trinidad and Tobago to estimate the causal impacts of 132 public secondary schools on various outcomes. It examines the relationship between school impacts on test scores and other outcomes like crime and labor market participation. The paper also explores how parental preferences align with these impacts, suggesting that parents value schools that improve both academic and non-academic outcomes.

paper_metadata.title: Elección de escuela y costos de cambio
paper_metadata.authors: Gallegos Mardones, Juan; Chumacero, Rómulo; Paredes, Ricardo
paper_metadata.summary: This paper examines the impact of switching costs on school choice within the context of Chile's voucher system. The study tracks students from fourth grade in 2000 to eighth grade in 2004 to determine if they changed schools and assesses the utility of the chosen school compared to new options available later. The findings reveal that switching costs are statistically significant and economically relevant, influencing the decision to change schools. The study highlights that marginal changes in utility levels are not significant enough to prompt a change in school choice, indicating a threshold effect. The paper underscores the importance of considering switching costs in understanding parental decision-making in school choice, particularly in systems like Chile's where vouchers are used to enhance competition and choice.

paper_metadata.title: School choice and information
paper_metadata.authors: Gómez, Daniel; Chumacero, Rómulo A.; Paredes, Ricardo D.
paper_metadata.summary: The paper examines the impact of public information on school quality on parental school choice in Chile. It uses data from 1996 and 2003, comparing periods when information on school performance was scarce versus when it was widely available. The study finds that school performance is a significant factor in school choice, even when parents are not fully informed about standardized test results. The publication of test results, however, increased the importance of performance in decision-making. The Chilean voucher system, introduced in 1981, assumes that competition among schools will improve educational quality. The paper suggests that while parents value quality, they may rely on other correlated information to assess school quality.

ccas_systems.notes: The paper discusses the Chilean voucher system, which includes public, subsidized, and private schools. The system allows for school choice but does not describe a centralized assignment process where applicants submit ranked preferences and receive a single offer.

paper_metadata.authors: Unknown
paper_metadata.summary: Unknown

paper_metadata.title: Design and Implementation of Student Verification System for Delta State University Nigeria (DELSU E-Verify)
paper_metadata.authors: Hampo, Johnpaul Anenechukwu
paper_metadata.summary: This research focuses on the design and implementation of a student verification system, DELSU E-Verify, for Delta State University in Nigeria. The system aims to address the issue of forgery and invalid studentship by providing a reliable method to verify student records and certificates. The system is designed to monitor studentship, maintain academic records, track malpractice, and verify university certificates. It employs a computerized approach to ensure the authenticity of student claims using matriculation numbers. The study highlights the importance of computer systems in enhancing verification processes and decision-making in educational institutions.

paper_metadata.title: Aftermarket Frictions and the Cost of Off-Platform Options in Centralized Assignment Mechanisms
paper_metadata.authors: Kapor, Adam; Karnani, Mohit; Neilson, Christopher
paper_metadata.summary: This paper examines the impacts of colleges' participation in Chile's centralized higher-education platform, focusing on welfare and human capital outcomes. The study leverages administrative data and two policy changes: a scholarship program introduction and the inclusion of more institutions, increasing on-platform slots by 40%. The findings show that platform expansion improves on-time graduation rates, particularly benefiting students from lower socioeconomic backgrounds. The paper develops a model to estimate aftermarket frictions and the effects of off-platform options, revealing that platform expansion significantly increases welfare and graduation rates. The study highlights the negative externalities of off-platform options and their impact on assignment system efficiency.

ccas_systems.notes: Chile's centralized higher-education assignment system uses the Deferred Acceptance algorithm. A recent policy change increased the number of on-platform institutions from 25 to 33, enhancing the number of available slots by 40%. This expansion led to improved on-time graduation rates and increased welfare, particularly for students from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.

paper_metadata.title: Winners and Losers? The Effect of Gaining and Losing Access to Selective Colleges on Education and Labor Market Outcomes
paper_metadata.authors: Black, Sandra E., Denning, Jeffrey T., Rothstein, Jesse
paper_metadata.summary: This paper examines the impact of the Texas Top Ten Percent rule (TTP) on college enrollment, graduation, and labor market outcomes. The TTP policy guarantees admission to any Texas public university for students in the top 10 percent of their high school class. The study uses administrative data from Texas to track students from high school through college and into the labor market. The findings show that students from disadvantaged high schools who gained access under the policy saw increased college enrollment and graduation rates. Conversely, students from more advantaged schools, who lost access to selective colleges, shifted to less-selective institutions but did not experience declines in overall college enrollment, graduation, or earnings. The policy benefited targeted students without adverse effects on those displaced. The paper highlights the trade-offs in selective college admissions and the importance of understanding the effects on both students who gain and lose access.

paper_metadata.title: Diversity Balance in Centralized Public School Admissions
paper_metadata.authors: Abdulkadiroğlu, Atila; Grigoryan, Aram
paper_metadata.summary: The paper addresses the challenge of balancing diversity objectives with priority rules in centralized public school admissions. It highlights the inherent conflict between achieving diversity and maintaining priority order, known as 'justified-envy.' The authors propose a reserves-based choice rule that minimizes priority violations while meeting diversity goals. This approach contrasts with traditional methods that prioritize stability and often ignore diversity objectives. The paper contributes to the literature by offering a model that optimally resolves these trade-offs, providing a transparent solution for school districts aiming to implement diversity policies without compromising on priority rules.

ccas_systems.notes: Chicago's selective high schools use income-based reserves, categorizing applicants into four income groups with 17.5% of seats reserved for each group.
Two districts, Northwest Brooklyn and Upper West Side, use reserves for low-income and low-achieving applicants, reserving 52% and 25% of seats respectively.

paper_metadata.title: Reforming School Choice Systems to Reduce Segregation in Schools: A Comparative Political Economy Study of Education Reform in Belgium, Chile, and the Netherlands
paper_metadata.authors: D’Agostino, TJ, Vernimmen, Jonas, Feldman, Audrey
paper_metadata.summary: This study examines desegregation policy reforms in Belgium, Chile, and the Netherlands, focusing on equity reforms to universal school choice systems. It analyzes reform trajectories, causal mechanisms of change, and efforts to institutionalize policies, identifying sources of resistance. The paper explores how shifts in political context, grassroots mobilization, and framing facilitated equity reforms despite opposition from non-state school networks and middle-class parents. The reforms, products of compromise, likely mitigated their impact on school segregation. The study employs legal and policy analysis, reviewing documents and literature, and uses causal process tracing for cross-case comparisons. It highlights the challenges of school choice policies in exacerbating segregation and the potential for well-designed policies to enhance equity and integration.

ccas_systems.notes: Belgium has implemented equity-oriented school choice reforms aimed at reducing segregation. The reforms were influenced by political shifts and grassroots mobilization, facing opposition from non-state school networks and middle-class parents.
Chile's school choice reforms aimed at desegregation were shaped by political context and grassroots efforts, with resistance from non-state school networks and middle-class parents.

The Netherlands has pursued school choice reforms to address segregation, influenced by political changes and grassroots mobilization, with opposition from non-state school networks and middle-class parents.

paper_metadata.title: Crosscutting Areas School Choice in Chile
paper_metadata.authors: Correa, José, Epstein, Natalie, Epstein, Rafael, Escobar, Juan, Rios, Ignacio, Aramayo, Nicolás, Bahamondes, Bastián, Bonet, Carlos, Castillo, Martin, Cristi, Andres, Epstein, Boris, Subiabre, Felipe
paper_metadata.summary: This paper discusses the design and implementation of a centralized school choice system in Chile, which is one of the largest of its kind globally, covering all grade levels from prekindergarten to 12th grade. The system was developed in response to the School Inclusion Law, which aimed to reduce social segregation by eliminating discriminatory selection criteria and copayments in publicly subsidized schools. The authors describe the challenges of implementing a system that favors the assignment of siblings to the same school, using a heuristic approach that adapts the deferred acceptance algorithm. The system also incorporates priority criteria such as sibling attendance, parental employment at the school, and returning students, while accommodating quotas for students with special needs, high academic performance, and disadvantaged backgrounds. The paper highlights the complexities of ensuring stability and fairness in the assignment process.

ccas_systems.notes: The Chilean school choice system is nationwide and covers all grade levels. It uses a deferred acceptance algorithm and incorporates priorities such as siblings, parental employment, and returning students. The system also addresses quotas for special needs, high academic performance, and disadvantaged students.

paper_metadata.title: Elite Universities and the Intergenerational Transmission of Human and Social Capital
paper_metadata.authors: Barrios-Fernández, Andrés; Neilson, Christopher; Zimmerman, Seth
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates whether elite colleges in Chile help talented students from lower-status backgrounds join the social elite or assist incumbent elites in maintaining their positions. Using intergenerationally-linked data and a regression discontinuity design, the study finds that elite colleges do both. Admission to elite programs transforms the social environment of lower-status individuals' children, making them more likely to attend high-status private schools and colleges, and to associate with high-status peers, although it does not affect academic achievement. The study highlights the persistence and interdependence of human and social capital across generations, showing that elite college attendance predicts upward social capital mobility. The research utilizes Chile's exam-based centralized admission system, which has been in place since the late 1960s, to estimate the causal effects of admission to elite degree programs. The findings suggest that while elite college attendance enhances social capital mobility, it does not significantly impact pre-college human capital, as measured by exam scores and grades.

paper_metadata.title: Elite Universities and the Intergenerational Transmission of Human and Social Capital
paper_metadata.authors: Barrios-Fernández, Andrés; Neilson, Christopher; Zimmerman, Seth
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates the role of elite universities in Chile in shaping the intergenerational transmission of social and human capital. Using five decades of linked data and a regression discontinuity design, the study examines how elite colleges affect social and educational outcomes across generations. It finds that while elite colleges significantly enhance social capital, they do not have the same effect on human capital. Children of parents who narrowly gain admission to elite programs are more likely to attend high-status high schools and elite colleges, but do not perform better academically than those whose parents narrowly miss admission. The paper highlights the role of elite private high schools and universities in perpetuating social capital and suggests that elite colleges contribute to social mobility, albeit unequally.

paper_metadata.title: Smart Matching Platforms and Heterogeneous Beliefs in Centralized School Choice
paper_metadata.authors: Arteaga, Felipe; Kapor, Adam J.; Neilson, Christopher A.; Zimmerman, Seth D.
paper_metadata.summary: This paper examines how beliefs about admissions chances influence school choice outcomes even in strategy-proof assignment systems. Using data from Chile and New Haven, the study shows that applicants' beliefs about their chances of admission significantly impact their search behavior and choice outcomes. The authors introduce 'smart matching platforms' that provide live feedback on admissions chances, helping applicants make more informed decisions. The study finds that these platforms reduce nonplacement risk and improve the quality of school placements. The research highlights the importance of supporting applicants in the search process, not just within the centralized system.

ccas_systems.notes: The Chilean school choice system is centralized and strategy-proof, with smart matching platforms providing live feedback on admissions chances to help applicants reduce nonplacement risk.
New Haven's school choice system is centralized and strategy-proof, with interventions like smart matching platforms to aid applicants in making informed decisions.

paper_metadata.title: School Choice and Educational Inequality in South Korea
paper_metadata.authors: Byun, Soo-Yong; Kim, Kyung-Keun; Park, Hyunjoon
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates the impact of the High School Equalization Policy (HSEP) in South Korea on educational inequality, using a nationally representative sample of eleventh-grade students. The study finds that the HSEP regions exhibit less between-school separation of students by socioeconomic status (SES) compared to non-HSEP regions. Additionally, student achievement is significantly influenced by a school's mean SES only in non-HSEP regions. The paper discusses the implications of these findings for school choice policies and educational inequality, highlighting the potential benefits of socioeconomically integrated schools for low SES students. The study contributes to the broader school choice debate by examining the effects of a random school assignment policy in a non-Western context.

ccas_systems.notes: The High School Equalization Policy (HSEP) assigns students to academic high schools within their residential area using a random computerized lottery. The policy aims to reduce educational inequality by promoting socioeconomically integrated schools. Critics argue it may lower student achievement by creating heterogeneous classrooms, while proponents believe it benefits low SES students.

paper_metadata.title: School Choice, Universal Vouchers and Native Flight from Local Schools
paper_metadata.authors: Schindler Rangvid, Beatrice
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates the phenomenon of native flight from local public schools in Copenhagen, Denmark, in response to high concentrations of immigrant pupils. Using comprehensive data from school registers, the study tests whether Danes are more likely to opt out of local public schools when the concentration of immigrant students exceeds 35%. The findings support the hypothesis that native Danes are more likely to leave local schools as immigrant concentration increases beyond this threshold. The study also highlights that only a minority of the immigrant population, specifically those who speak Danish at home, exhibit similar opting-out behavior. The research contributes to the literature by examining the joint opting-out effect from public to both public and private alternative schools, utilizing precise data on school catchment areas and ethnic composition. It also explores the school choices of immigrant families, a dimension often overlooked in previous studies. The paper underscores the role of ethnic segregation in shaping school choice and its implications for educational policy.

ccas_systems.notes: The paper discusses the opting-out behavior of native Danes from local public schools in response to high immigrant concentrations, but does not provide specific details on the coordinated choice and assignment systems in place.

paper_metadata.title: Effects of Free Choice Among Public Schools
paper_metadata.authors: Lavy, Victor
paper_metadata.summary: This paper investigates the impact of a school choice program in Tel-Aviv, Israel, which ended an inter-district busing integration program and allowed students free choice among public schools. Using difference-in-differences and regression discontinuity designs, the study finds that school choice significantly reduces dropout rates and increases cognitive achievements of high school students. It also improves behavioral outcomes such as teacher-student relationships, social acclimation, and satisfaction, while reducing violence and classroom disruption. The program, implemented in 1994, initially affected district 9, the city's largest, and used adjacent districts and cities as comparison groups. The findings suggest that the improvements in dropout rates and matriculation outcomes can be causally linked to the choice program. The study also explores the effects of school choice on behavioral outcomes, using longitudinal data to show improvements in violence, disruption, and social acclimation. The Tel-Aviv program is compared to similar reforms in the United States, such as those in Mecklenburg County, North Carolina.

ccas_systems.notes: The Tel-Aviv School Choice Program allowed students to choose schools within and outside their district, ending a previous busing integration program. The program was initially implemented in district 9, with adjacent districts and cities serving as comparison groups. The study finds significant improvements in dropout rates, cognitive achievements, and behavioral outcomes.

paper_metadata.title: Approaches to Education Market Design
paper_metadata.authors: Narita, Yusuke
paper_metadata.summary: This thesis explores methods to enhance education markets by analyzing data from these markets. Chapter 1 examines how families make school choices in NYC's high school choice system, revealing that choices evolve as families learn about schools. An empirical model is developed to understand demand changes due to learning and switching costs, suggesting that demand changes undermine the welfare of initial matches. Dynamic mechanisms are proposed to accommodate choice changes, showing that centralized market benefits depend on demand-side frictions. Chapter 2 investigates education production post-admission, focusing on the use of lotteries in oversubscribed schools to study the impact of school admission on outcomes like test scores. It provides conditions under which random assignments are effectively extracted for empirical research. Chapter 3, co-authored with Abdulkadiroglu, Angrist, and Pathak, develops strategies to utilize lottery-generated variations for impact evaluation, finding significant achievement gains from charter school attendance in Denver. Overall, the thesis highlights the importance of considering demand-side dynamics and the use of random assignments in evaluating educational outcomes.

ccas_systems.notes: The NYC high school choice system allows families to rank schools, with a centralized algorithm assigning students based on preferences and available slots. The study highlights how families' choices evolve post-initial match due to learning and switching costs, impacting the welfare of the initial assignment.

The study uses lottery-generated variation in Denver's charter school admissions to evaluate the impact on student achievement, finding significant gains. The system involves random assignment, which is used to assess educational outcomes.

paper_metadata.title: Preference Discovery in University Admissions: The Case for Dynamic Multioffer Mechanisms
paper_metadata.authors: Grenet, Julien; He, YingHua; Kübler, Dorothea
paper_metadata.summary: The paper challenges the assumption in matching literature that agents have full information on their preferences, using Germany's university admissions as a case study. It documents that early non-exploding offers are more likely to be accepted by students, indicating costly preference discovery. The study proposes a dynamic multioffer mechanism that batches early offers, improving matching efficiency by informing students of offer availability before they finalize their preferences. The paper provides empirical evidence from Germany's Dialogorientiertes Serviceverfahren (DoSV), which allows students to hold multiple offers, and suggests that this mechanism could be more efficient than the traditional Deferred Acceptance (DA) mechanism.

ccas_systems.notes: Germany uses the Dialogorientiertes Serviceverfahren (DoSV) for university admissions, which implements the first stages of the Gale-Shapley algorithm in real time, allowing students to hold multiple offers. This system is designed to help students discover their preferences more efficiently by providing early information on available offers.

paper_metadata.title: Research Design with Weighted Lotteries in School Choice
paper_metadata.authors: Abdulkadiroğlu, Atila; Back, Inkoo
paper_metadata.summary: The paper discusses the use of weighted lotteries in school choice systems, particularly focusing on the deferred acceptance algorithm. It highlights how school districts in the United States implement diversity policies through weighted lotteries, granting higher priority to certain groups, reserving seats, or weighing lottery numbers. The study extends previous theories by Abdulkadiroğlu et al. (2017) to include these diversity policies. The paper also addresses the quasi-experimental variation in assignments due to nonrandom preferences and priorities, which can lead to omitted variable bias. The authors propose empirical strategies to exploit random assignments in school choice mechanisms, focusing on the estimation of applicants' probabilities of assignment. The paper provides examples from Colorado, Chicago, and New York City, illustrating how these regions implement diversity policies in their school choice systems.

ccas_systems.notes: Colorado uses a weighted lottery system to favor economically disadvantaged students and those with disabilities. The system was adopted to address disparities in charter school admissions.
Chicago's selective high schools reserve 17.5% of seats for each of four income groups to ensure diversity.
New York City reserves seats for low-income applicants in middle school admissions to promote diversity.

paper_metadata.title: The Welfare Effects of Coordinated Assignment: Evidence from the New York City High School Match
paper_metadata.authors: Abdulkadiroğlu, Atila; Agarwal, Nikhil; Pathak, Parag A.
paper_metadata.summary: This paper examines the impact of the transition from an uncoordinated to a coordinated school assignment system in New York City high schools. The previous uncoordinated system allowed students to apply to five programs and receive multiple offers, leading to inefficiencies and a significant number of students being administratively assigned to less desirable schools. The new coordinated system, implemented in 2003, uses a deferred acceptance algorithm allowing students to rank up to 12 programs, resulting in a single offer. The study finds that the coordinated system significantly improved student welfare, particularly for those who were previously administratively assigned. The new system captures 80% of the potential welfare gains compared to a no-choice neighborhood system. The paper also discusses the broader implications of coordinated assignment systems, which have been adopted in other cities following NYC's example.

ccas_systems.notes: The NYC high school assignment system transitioned to a coordinated mechanism in 2003, using a student-proposing deferred acceptance algorithm. Students can rank up to 12 programs, and the system generates a single offer, reducing mismatches and administrative placements.
